response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
    "opinion": "I've been through a lot of these conversations lately, especially since my husband is in charge of our household now. He works as an electrician in New York City and we're both anxious people so when someone brings up the vaccine on social media—like that post about them being unsafe or something—I get worried right away. But I try to stay calm because it's important for me to be able to discuss this with him properly.

First off, you gotta know what information is reliable. There are lots of fake news stories floating around online these days, and my husband tells me sometimes he sees posts that make people nervous about getting vaccinated against COVID-19. I don't have much formal education myself—we stopped after eighth grade—but reading novels helps me understand complex topics better than just hearing rumors.

I remember last summer during the heatwave when temperatures were through the roof in New York, we went hiking together near Central Park because my dad used to love nature walks before he passed away. Now thinking back, maybe that's why I feel more comfortable talking about science topics like vaccines if they're explained simply without too many technical terms.

Also being part of our local Hindu community means sometimes people share misinformation during religious gatherings or through WhatsApp groups—my husband attends the temple services while working late shifts—but it helps to stick with trusted sources. For example, my sister from India who works in healthcare keeps us updated on reliable information about COVID vaccines.",
    "reasoning_summary": "I started by thinking about how anxiety might affect me when encountering conflicting opinions online or in person.
Then I considered my limited education level and how reading novels helps process complex topics.
My husband's profession as an electrician provides a parallel to needing clear instructions without jargon for understanding scientific concepts.
My moderate conscientiousness means being prepared with information from reliable sources but not overly rigid; agreeing with basic facts while remaining open-minded about nuances.
High extraversion motivates me to engage in discussions despite anxiety because I enjoy social interactions, like sharing info during family meals or temple gatherings.
Low neuroticism prevents me from getting too stressed by the topic itself.",
    "tone": "Calm yet concerned",
    "citations": ["Local Hindu community sources", "Personal knowledge"],
    "safety_notes": "None"
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've been through a lot of these conversations lately, especially since my husband is in charge of our household now. He works as an electrician in New York City and we're both anxious people so when someone brings up the vaccine on social media—like that post about them being unsafe or something—I get worried right away. But I try to stay calm because it's important for me to be able to discuss this with him properly.

First off, you gotta know what information is reliable. There are lots of fake news stories floating around online these days, and my husband tells me sometimes he sees posts that make people nervous about getting vaccinated against COVID-19. I don't have much formal education myself—we stopped after eighth grade—but reading novels helps me understand complex topics better than just hearing rumors.

I remember last summer during the heatwave when temperatures were through the roof in New York, we went hiking together near Central Park because my dad used to love nature walks before he passed away. Now thinking back, maybe that's why I feel more comfortable talking about science topics like vaccines if they're explained simply without too many technical terms.

Also being part of our local Hindu community means sometimes people share misinformation during religious gatherings or through WhatsApp groups—my husband attends the temple services while working late shifts—but it helps to stick with trusted sources. For example, my sister from India who works in healthcare keeps us updated on reliable information about COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I started by thinking about how anxiety might affect me when encountering conflicting opinions online or in person.
Then I considered my limited education level and how reading novels helps process complex topics.
My husband's profession as an electrician provides a parallel to needing clear instructions without jargon for understanding scientific concepts.
My moderate conscientiousness means being prepared with information from reliable sources but not overly rigid; agreeing with basic facts while remaining open-minded about nuances.
High extraversion motivates me to engage in discussions despite anxiety because I enjoy social interactions, like sharing info during family meals or temple gatherings.
Low neuroticism prevents me from getting too stressed by the topic itself.
